ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chicken nuggets as a prepared food item; SNOMED CT coverage for processed snack foods is limited.

serving.description: 6 pieces of chicken nuggets with seasoning
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of jumbo chicken nuggets is approximately 6 pieces (150 g), providing around 489 kcal, 24.8 g protein, 33.9 g fat, and 21.5 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken nuggets, breaded, cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 21040)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010386 at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (dietary_fibre_g, saturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for breaded fried chicken nuggets (NDB 21040) to validate plausibility. HPB values are treated as primary; USDA used for gap-filling and sanity checking.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on breaded fried chicken products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jumbo chicken nuggets in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 66 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: breaded and fried chicken products with wheat-flour coating, which fall in the medium GI range (GI ~65–70) due to the refined wheat breadcrumb coating. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × available carbohydrate per serving (14.3 g/100g × 150 g serving = 21.45 g) / 100 = 14.2 g available carb per serving → GL = 66 × 14.15 / 100 ≈ 9.3. Corrected GL per 150 g serving = 66 × (14.3 × 1.5) / 100 = 66 × 21.45 / 100 = 14.16 / 100 × 66 = 14.16 → recalculated: GL = (66 × 21.45) / 100 = 14.16 ≈ 14.2. Note: GL reported as 14.2 per 150 g serving. Value set to 14.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 708 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit jumbo chicken nuggets; a single 150 g serving delivers 1,062 mg sodium (approximately 47% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and pairing with dipping sauces such as ketchup or BBQ sauce will further increase sodium load substantially.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High fat content (22.6 g/100 g), trans fat (0.33 g/100 g), and medium GI breadcrumb coating with negligible dietary fibre (0.67 g/100 g) impair glycaemic control and insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid deep-fried breaded chicken nuggets; the combination of medium-GI refined wheat coating, high total fat, and trans fat worsens postprandial glucose excursions and insulin resistance, and the near-absent fibre provides no glycaemic buffering — baked or air-fried skinless chicken is a strongly preferred alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat (0.33 g/100 g), high sodium (708 mg/100 g), and high total fat (22.6 g/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol, lower HDL cholesterol, and raise blood pressure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid jumbo chicken nuggets; the trans fat content raises LDL and lowers HDL cholesterol, the high sodium exacerbates hypertension risk, and the cholesterol content (50 mg/100 g) adds further atherogenic burden — regular consumption is inconsistent with heart-healthy dietary patterns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.33 g/100 g) and deep-frying-generated advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) are strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried breaded chicken nuggets; trans fat activates TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways, and AGEs generated during deep-frying directly worsen joint inflammation with no anti-inflammatory nutrients present to offset this burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jumbo Chicken Nuggets are large, bite-sized pieces of seasoned chicken meat encased in a light wheat-flour breadcrumb coating and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains tender and juicy. This product is a Western-origin fast-food staple widely consumed in Singapore as a snack, lunch, or dinner item, typically served with dipping sauces such as sweet chilli, ketchup, or BBQ sauce. Per 100 g, the product delivers 326 kcal, 16.5 g protein, 22.6 g fat (including 3.33 g saturated fat and 0.33 g trans fat), and 14.3 g carbohydrate with negligible sugar (0.08 g) and very low dietary fibre (0.67 g). Sodium is very high at 708 mg per 100 g, making this product clinically unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are pro-inflammatory, and the trans fat content worsens insulin resistance and raises LDL cholesterol. A standard serving of 6 pieces (approximately 150 g) provides around 489 kcal and 1,062 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 66 (medium) based on the refined wheat breadcrumb coating. This is an ultra-processed food product not recommended for regular consumption in health-conscious dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken nuggets?
Is chicken nuggets suitable for hypertension?
Are chicken nuggets high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Primary macronutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record G00010386 at confidence 0.95. Secondary micronutrients (dietary fibre, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) from the same HPB record supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for plausibility validation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for breaded fried chicken products, clearly documented as an estimate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific HPB product not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for breaded fried chicken nuggets as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source record for this product and are not available with sufficient confidence from secondary sources for this specific formulation. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed as zero per HPB source record — chicken nuggets contain no meaningful vitamin C. GI value of 66 is an analogous estimate based on breaded fried chicken products in the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product. GL per 150 g serving recalculated as (66 × 21.45) / 100 = 14.2. Halal status noted as likely true given Singapore food service context but not formally verified from a certification body — flagged for review. Trans fat value of 0.33 g/100 g sourced from HPB record at confidence 0.70 (grabfood-derived); this value is plausible for deep-fried breaded chicken but should be verified against manufacturer specification if available.
